coredata.dc:title: Structure, morphology and modelling studies of polyvinylalcohol nanocomposites reinforced with nickel oxide nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Y. Ravi
Deshmukh, Kalim
Ali, M. Mohamed Naseer
Abhijay, Gade
Al-Onazi, Wedad A.
Al-Mohaimeed, Amal M.
Pasha, S.K. Khadheer
coredata.dc:description: Nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles (NPs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) reinforced polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) nanocomposite films were prepared using a solution casting technique. The physicochemical characteristics of PVA/NiO/GQDs (PNG) nanocomposite films were studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The obtained PNG nanocomposite films showed good mechanical flexibility and improved tensile strength. The influence of nanofiller concentrations on PNG nanocomposite film. The obtained results demonstrate an increase in the activation energy (Ea) up to PNG3 upon increasing the GQDs concentration and thereafter, its decreases. The fundamental interactions of the constituents of PNG nanocomposite film were investigated using density functional theory (DFT). This study on electronic structure reveals that the PVA model indirectly interacts with GQDs through the NiO model. This configuration is favoured in terms of interaction energy (−78 kJ/mol) compared to the one in which PVA interacts directly with the GQDs model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiO
GQDs
PVA
Polymer nanocomposites
Structural model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structures and energetics of PNG model

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by RGEMS project, VIT-AP/2021/VC/IIC/RGEMS. The authors extend their appreciation to the researchers supporting project number (RSP-2021/247) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis protocol of PNG nanocomposite films via solution casting process.
FT-IR spectra of PVA/NiO/GQDs nanocomposite films with different composition of NiO and GQDs (a) PG (b) PN (c) PNG1 (d) PNG2 (e) PNG3 (f) PNG4 (g) PNG5 and (h) PNG6.

(I) XRD pattern of NiO NPs and PNG nanocomposite films with different composition of NiO NPs and GQDs (a) NiO NPs (b) PG (c) PN (d) PNG1 (e) PNG2 (f) PNG3 (g) PNG4 (h) PNG5 and (i) PNG6 (II) Zoomed-in image of the plane (002) in the 2θ range of 21–25 °C corresponds to GQDs.
FESEM images (a) PG (b) PN and (c) PNG1.
TGA thermograms of (a) PG (b) PN (c) PNG1 (d) PNG2 (e) PNG3 (f) PNG4 (g) PNG5 and (h) PNG6.
Determination of activation energy (Ea) for PG, PN, and PNG1-PNG6 from coats and Redfern method.

B3LYP/lanl2dz optimised structures of PNG nanocomposite model in (a) configuration 1, (b) configuration 2. In configuration 1 PVA model is in plane with GQD and the NiO model is above the GQD's plane but in configuration 2 both PVA and NiO models are above the GQD's plane. During optimization cubit structure of NiO model is found to be deviated due to non-constraint optimization of the entire PNG model.

The δinter iso-surface maps for the intermolecular interaction between PVA, NiO and GQD in two different configurations (Configuration 1 (a) and Configuration 2 (b)). Iso-surfaces are plotted with iso value of 0.01 a. u.
RDG (r) vs. sign (λ2)ρ graphs for the two configurations; (a) Configuration 1, and (b) Configuration 2.

Constituent models considered for PNG nanocomposite modelling. (a) basic model for PVA. The terminal ends of PVA repetitive unit are capped with a methyl group. (b) Graphene Quantum Dot model with edge substituted hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. (c) Smallest possible cubic unit of NiO model.
PVA, NiO and GQDs compositions used for preparation of PNG nanocomposite films.
Activation energy (Ea in kJ/mol) for PG, PN, PNG1-PNG6.

head.ce:title: Structure, morphology and modelling studies of polyvinylalcohol nanocomposites reinforced with nickel oxide nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, The New College (Autonomous), University of Madras, Chennai, 600014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O.Box 22452, Riyadh, 11495, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O.Box 22452, Riyadh 11495, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles (NPs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) reinforced polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) nanocomposite films were prepared using a solution casting technique. The physicochemical characteristics of PVA/NiO/GQDs (PNG) nanocomposite films were studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The obtained PNG nanocomposite films showed good mechanical flexibility and improved tensile strength. The influence of nanofiller concentrations on PNG nanocomposite film. The obtained results demonstrate an increase in the activation energy (Ea) up to PNG3 upon increasing the GQDs concentration and thereafter, its decreases. The fundamental interactions of the constituents of PNG nanocomposite film were investigated using density functional theory (DFT). This study on electronic structure reveals that the PVA model indirectly interacts with GQDs through the NiO model. This configuration is favoured in terms of interaction energy (−78 kJ/mol) compared to the one in which PVA interacts directly with the GQDs model.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, polymers have attracted great attention in a broad range of applications in various technological areas. Traditionally, poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) has been widely used amidst a range of conducting and non-conducting polymers owing to its availability in different molecular weights and cost-effectiveness. Furthermore, it exhibits significant film forming characteristic, good water solubility, and easy processability. Also, it is non-toxic, biocompatible, biodegradable and illustrates good resistance to chemical effect (Adhikari and Majumdar, 2004; Ningaraju et al., 2018; Thangamani et al., 2017). Moreover, it demonstrates excellent optical, electrical, and thermal properties. These remarkable properties make PVA an ideal candidate for many applications that include biomedical devices, drug delivery, membrane technology, fuel cells, and gas sensors (Karuppasamy et al., 2021a,b; Davar et al., 2018; Nangia et al., 2019). To enhance the properties of PVA, various nanofillers are usually added. The changes in the polymer properties occur due to the large surface area of the nanofillers (Theerthagiri et al., 2021; Karuppasamy et al., 2021; Thangamani et al., 2017; Davar et al., 2018). One such nanofiller is nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles (NPs), a p-type semiconducting metal oxide with a wide energy bandgap of 3.6–4.0 eV. It has excellent properties such as high chemical stability, high melting point (1960 °C) and excellent electrical and optical properties. The electrical conductivity varies due to the change in the charge carriers of NiO nanoparticles. The variation in NiO structures is due to the non-stoichiometric effect that makes them suitable for solar cells, electrochromic devices, optoelectronic and sensing applications (Theerthagiri et al., 2021; Hotovy et al., 2002; Tian et al., 2016; Gomaa et al., 2018).

On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) belong to the family of carbon materials. They have remarkable electrical properties with tunable optical and electrical properties as compared to graphene. They are zero-dimensional materials obtained from two-dimensional graphene, which results in edge effect and quantum confinement makes them very good candidates for diverse applications (Tian et al., 2018; Raeyani et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2020). Edge atoms in GQDs lead to a good interaction with the surrounding molecules compared to graphene due to the large surface to volume ratio (Zhang et al., 2012). The bandgap tuning property and electron transport ability with a change in the size of GQDs can be used in sensing (Feng et al., 2019). Hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl pendant groups serve as a linker in GQDs, making them possible to combine with polymers such as PVA. Due to proper dispersion in the polymer solution, NiO and GQDs are considered potential candidates to fabricate novel hybrid materials for various applications. On this context, NiO and GQDs were used as nano fillers and integrated into the PVA matrix (Adhikari and Majumdar, 2004). In this study, an attempt has been made to synthesise and characterise PNG nanocomposite film by incorporating various composition of NiO and GQDs into the PVA matrix. This investigation also explores the possible physical forces involved in stabilising the formed complex from the results of density functional theory (DFT). In recent days, first principle DFT studies have been widely used in the field of basic research to applications due to the development of efficient algorithms and codes (Hafner et al., 2006; Narasimhan, 2020).

PVA was purchased from SD Fine Chem. Ltd, India with an average molecular weight of 85,000–1,24,000 g/mol−1 with the degree of hydrolysis of 86–89 %. NiO was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. NiO NPs were about <50 nm size with a molecular weight of 74.69 g/mol. GQDs of nearly15 nm and thickness between 0.5 and 2 nm were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL) Pvt. Ltd, India. Distilled water was used as a solvent throughout the work.

In a typical PNG nanocomposite film synthesis, various compositions of NiO and GQDs were considered, as given in Table 1 . The PNG nanocomposite film was prepared using a solution casting process, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, PVA was dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water under constant stirring (200 rpm) at 60 °C for 3 h to obtain a homogeneous solution. Also, NiO NPs and GQDs were dispersed separately in 20 ml of distilled water using ultrasonication for an hour. The sonicated NiO NPs were added to the PVA solution under stirring. After 1 h, the dispersed GQDs were added to PVA/NiO (PN) complex solution. The resultant PNG (PVA/NiO/GQDs) solution was continuously stirred at room temperature overnight and subsequently transferred to a Teflon Petri dish for drying at 60 °C for 24 h in a hot air oven. Finally, the PNG nanocomposite films were peeled off from the Petri dish and further characterised.

FTIR spectra of PG (PVA/GQD), PN (PVA/NiO) and PNG1-PNG6 (PVA/NiO/GQD) nanocomposite films were obtained using Fourier Transform Infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, IRAffinity-1, Japan) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction patterns of pure NiO, PG, PN, PNG1-PNG6 nanocomposite films were obtained using Bruker AXS D8 focus advanced X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan, Tokyo). The scans were taken in the 2θ range from 10° to 90° using CuKα radiation of wavelength 1.54060 Å (λ)with the scanning speed and step size of 1° min−1 and 0.01°, respectively. The morphology of PG, PN, and PNG1-PNG6 nanocomposite films surfaces was studied using field emission scanning electron microscopy (Quanta 200 FEG FE-SEM). An accelerating voltage of 15 kV was applied to obtain SEM images. For SEM images, the samples were coated with gold for better electrode contact. Thermal stability of PVA/NiO/GQDs nanocomposite films was evaluated using a TGA Instrument (Model No. STA7200, Hitachi HTG, Japan). Samples were heated to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

To understand the fundamental interactions between the constituents (Scheme 1 ) of PNG nanocomposites, DFT studies were carried out. A cubic model of NiO structure shown in Scheme 1c was coampred with Persson's work (Persson, 2014). PVA and GQDs were prepared using Argus Lab (Thompson, 2004). In the present study, PNG nanocomposite molecular models have been generated in two different configurations. In the first configuration, the PVA model is in the same plane as the GQDs and NiO particle is above the GQDs. In the second configuration, PVA and NiO models occur above the GQDs plane. Geometry optimization of these configurations was carried out using B3LYP exchange-correlation functional (Becke, 1993; Lee et al., 1988; Vosko et al., 1980; Stephens et al.) with lanl2dz (Dunning Jr. and Hay, 1977; Wadt and Hay, 1985; Hay and Wadt, 1985) basis function. During optimization, the models were allowed to relax completely. To confirm the obtained optimised structures as energetically minima, frequency analysis was carried out with the same level of theory and found that all the obtained frequencies were found to be positive for both configurations. Signature intra and intermolecular forces between the PNG nanocomposite constituents can be established through the recently developed method viz the Independent Gradient Model (IGM) (Lefebvre et al., 2017). Data were computed for IGM and Relative Density Gradient (RDG) analyses using Multiwfn software (Lu and Chen, 2012). The iso-surfaces of PNG nanocomposite models were rendered using visual molecualar dynamics (VMD) 1.9.3 (Humphrey et al., 1996).

FTIR is a promising technique to characterise the functional groups and molecular interactions in the materials. In the present study, this technique is used to understand and characterise the PNG nanocomposite films. Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of PG, PN and PNG nanocomposite film at various concentrations. A broad region between 3016 cm−1 and 3668 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching band of the hydroxyl group. The presence of a broad peak around 3284 cm−1 is due to the stretching of the O–H bond of the adsorbed H2O (Ningaraju et al., 2018). The peaks at 2918 cm−1 and 2939 cm−1 are observed due to C–H symmetric and asymmetric stretching respectively (Ningaraju et al., 2018; Thangamani et al., 2017; Kharazmi et al., 2015). B3LYP/landl2dz calculated symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrational frequencies were found at 2913 cm−1 and 2963 cm−1 respectively and the DFT results are comparable to the experimentally observed C–H stretching frequencies. The peak at 1724 cm−1 belongs to CO stretching band of the carboxylic acid of GQDs (Raeyani et al., 2016). A shifting of bands was observed from 1421 cm−1 to 1429 cm−1 corresponding to the bending vibration of the O–H group upon an increase in the concentration of NiO NPs. This indicates the predominant interaction of hydroxyl group of PVA with NiO through hydrogen bonding (Ningaraju et al., 2018). The peaks observed at 1082 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1 correspond to C–O stretching of PVA backbone (Thangamani et al., 2017). The peaks at 416 cm−1 and 468 cm−1 can be related to Ni–O stretching vibration band of NiO NPs (Babu et al., 2013; Sudhasree et al., 2014; Sabouri et al., 2019). Therefore, the observed FT-IR spectroscopic features establish the incorporated functional groups of PNG nanocomposite.

XRD pattern of the bare NiO NPs and PG, PN, PNG1-PNG6 nanocomposite films are depicted in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(I) shows the XRD pattern of PNG film, indicating a characteristic broad peak of the PVA crystalline phase at 2θ = 19.65°, which corresponds to (101) plane (Mohanapriya et al., 2017). A wide (002) peak observed in the 2θ range from 21° to 25° is due to the GQDs with low intensity, and it could not be observed in the XRD pattern of PN (Dong et al., 2012). The broader diffraction peak is attributed to the nano size of GQDs, with only a few layers of graphene sheets (Kumar et al., 2017). This indicates that the peak of GQDs is quite weak in the nanocomposites even at the higher concentration of GQDs (Rahimi et al., 2018; Wang and Zhao, 2013). The diffraction peaks at 2θ of 37.45°, 43.51°, 63.11°, 75.65°, and 79.56° can be assigned to the face-centered cubic crystalline structure with many diffraction planes, i.e., (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) of the NiO structure, respectively (Gomaa et al., 2018). A peak at 2θ = 43.51° could also be observed, which corresponds to (200) plane of crystalline NiO particle (Thangamani et al., 2015). The XRD pattern shown in Fig. 3(II) represents the enlarged (002) peak.

The dispersion of nanofillers in the polymer matrix is one of the most important parameters for producing polymer nanocomposite film with enhanced properties (Pasha et al., 2017). Fig. 4 shows the FESEM morphology of (a) PG (b) PN and (c) PNG nanocomposite films. Fig. 4(a and b) reveal that GQDs and NiO NPs are attached to the PVA surface. Similarly, Fig. 4(c) shows that GQDs and NiO nanofillers' combination are agglomerate on the PVA surface (Le-Qing et al., 2014). The PNG combination is almost spherical with the particle size in the range of 50–250 nm, which is consistent with the particle size derived from the Scherrer equation.

TGA analysis was used to study the thermal stability of PG, PN, and PNG1-PNG6. TGA thermograms, given in Fig. 5 , show four stages of degradation of PNG. The first stage of degradation was noticed when the temperature rises from room temperature to 96 °C due to the evaporation of absorbed H2O molecules (Deshmukh et al., 2015). At the same time, the overall weight loss, in this case, was found to be around 6 %. The second stage of degradation appears in the temperature range of 96–291 °C is due to the splitting of polymer chains, and the corresponding weight loss is about 64 % (Thangamani et al., 2017). The third degradation stage appears in the temperature range between 291 and 460 °C, and the weight loss is about 15 %, which is due to the decomposition of the polymer backbone (Reddy et al., 2019). Moreover, a slight weight loss in the fourth stage appears when the temperature changes from 460 to 650 °C, due to the desorption of excess oxygen and polymer chain from the sample due to the exothermal reaction, resulting in the burning of GQDs fragments in the nanocomposites. In other word, a carbonaceous residue were present in the nanocomposites (Dubey and Kaurav, 2019; Kharangarh et al., 2021; Deshmukh et al., 2016). The observed weight loss, in this case, is about 11 %. The degradation process is found to extend up to four stages due to the presence of NiO, signifying that NiO contributes to the thermal stability of the nanocomposite. This effect arises as NiO has high thermal stability, good chemical stability and high melting point (Tian et al., 2016). This observation is substantiated from the trends of TGA thermograms of PG and other samples, where a variation in the fourth stage of weight loss is due to the absence of NiO NPs.

The activation energy in which the order of reaction parameter can be calculated using Coats and Redfern method (Abthagir et al., 2004). The related Eq. (1) and (2) are shown below.
(1)
log
[
1 −
( 1 − α )
1 − n
T 2
( 1 − n )
]
(2)
L o g
[
− log
( 1 − α )
/
T 2
]

Plotting Eq. (2) against 1000/T, leading to a linear trendwith a slope (Ea/2.303 R) from which activation energy could be calculated. Fig. 6 shows the plots obtained from kinetic analysis of TGA data of PNG. The Ea of PG, PN, and PNG1-PNG6 nanocomposite is ~26 kJ/mol, calculated using the above Eq. (2). For all the samples, Ea values were almost similar, as shown in Table 2 . The Ea values show increasing trends with the increasing concentration of GQDs up to PNG3, and then it decreases with a further increase in the concentration of GQDs. These results demonstrate that the Ea of PNG3 is the highest due to its high thermal stability compared to other samples (Gul et al., 2013). According to Jonscher and Ansari, the present results shows that electron mobility is dominate in all the samples. As a result of the electron mobility, the addition of NiO and GQDs to the host PVA matrix may improve the electrical properties (Jonscher and Ansari, 1970; Abdullah et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures and energetics of PNG model
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between the constituent groups of PNG nanocomposite can be analysed from the structural features of the models obtained using DFT calculations. The optimised structures of the PNG models are shown in Fig. 7 . Among the models considered, “out of plane model” (Configuration 1; Fig. 7(a)) is energetically more favourable than “in-plane model” (Configuration 2; Fig. 7(b)) by 78 kJ/mol. To understand the interaction between individual molecules of the composite, IGM analysis was carried out, and the observed result is shown in Fig. 8 . From this figure, it is seen that the NiO fragment directly interacts with GQDs (Fig. 8(b)) and PVA is not directly interacting with GQDs but with the metal-ligand. It is interesting to note from the results of non-covalent interaction (NCI) analyses, shown in Fig. 9 , that the spike on the positive side centered around +0.02 is more crowded than its counterpart due to steric interaction in configuration 1. This reveals that the steric energy increases the potential energy of the configuration 1, as evidenced by the RDG plot (Fig. 9(a)). Furthermore, it is seen from configuration 2 that the spike between −0.02 and −0.01 appears relatively denser than that of configuration 1, indicating that the interaction is more favourable in configuration 2 than in configuration 1.

PVA nanocomposite films reinforced with NiO NPs and GQDs (PG, PN, PNG1-PNG6)were synthesised by the solution casting technique. The physicochemical characteristics and thermal studies were carried out using FTIR, XRD, FESEM and TGA. The XRD patterns revealed the formation of PNG nanocomposite films. All the functional groups in the PNG nanocomposite films were confirmed through FTIR spectroscopic analysis. GQDs and NiO nanofillers' complex formation is confirmed through FESEM images and complemented by DFT calculations. The thermal degradation studies using TGA show that the degradation extends up to the fourth stage due to the incorporation of NiO nanofiller as it has high thermal stability. For all the samples, Ea values were also calculated using the Redfern coats method, and the highest Ea value of ~25.99 kJ/mol was observed for PNG3. This indicates that PNG3 is a thermally more stable than the other prepared nanocomposites. Furthermore, The DFT models agree very well with the experimentally observed PNG assembly. The excellent properties of NiO and GQDs as well as the strong mobility of electrons within PNG nanocomposites, make them the most are most promising materials for energy storage and sensing applications.

Y. Ravi Kumar – Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Kalim Deshmukh – Writing – review & editing. M. Mohamed Naseer Ali – Writing – review & editing. Gade Abhijay – Writing – review & editing. Wedad A. Al-Onazi – Writing – review & editing. Amal M. Al-Mohaimeed – Project administration, Investigation, Supervision. S.K. Khadheer Pasha - Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by RGEMS project, VIT-AP/2021/VC/IIC/RGEMS. The authors extend their appreciation to the researchers supporting project number (RSP-2021/247) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
